text:
## Overview
This page defines Redwood Inference’s company-wide standard for:
- What counts as a performance regression vs. measurement noise.
- Minimum sample sizes and repeatability requirements.
- Pass/fail thresholds used by automated perf gating (PR checks, nightly canaries, and release-branch gates).
- Required artifacts and triage expectations when a regression is detected.

This policy is designed to support the “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” program and applies broadly to the serving runtime, kernels, scheduler/batching, and benchmark harness changes.

**Owner:** Observability (Engineering) in partnership with Serving Runtime and SRE.

---

## Scope
### In scope
This policy applies to any change that can affect latency/throughput/cost-relevant behavior for production inference:
- Serving runtime changes (prefill/decode scheduling, KV cache management, batching...

text:
## Status
**Accepted** (published)

## Decision
We will ship **three customer-facing batching default profiles** aligned to **latency SLO tiers**:
- **fast** (tail-latency protection)
- **standard** (balanced default)
- **cost-efficient** (throughput/$ optimization)

Defaults will be **workload-aware within the tier** (using a small set of runtime signals such as prompt-length buckets, streaming vs non-streaming, and observed concurrency/queue pressure), but we will **not** ship **per-tenant auto-tuned batching** in the initial release.

We will provide a clear **override hierarchy** and **reason codes** so operators can understand and control effective behavior:
1) Customer override (Dedicated/Enterprise) 
2) Route/model policy selection (control plane)
3) Tier default (fast/standard/cost-efficient)
4) Safety fallback / overload mode constraints (runtime)

Redwood’s infere...

title: Contract Gates and Canary Mesh Guidelines for Service APIs (2026)
preview:
Summary:

It prescribes a single reproducible flow from code change to gradual traffic rollout that enforces behavioral compatibility, performance/observability gates, and automatic rollback criteria....
text:
Summary:
This document defines the contract validation, runtime gating, and canary mesh requirements for Redwood service APIs. It prescribes a single reproducible flow from code change to gradual traffic rollout that enforces behavioral compatibility, performance/observability gates, and automatic rollback criteria. Goal: reduce customer-facing regressions caused by API changes while enabling fast, iterative service delivery.

Who this is for:
- Service owners and backend engineers implementing HTTP/gRPC APIs
- SDK and client library maintainers
- Release engineers and SREs configuring rollout pipelines
- QA and automated testing teams responsible for contract tests

Principles:
1) Consumer-driven validation: tests authored from client expectations (integration harnesses, recorded interactions) are authoritative for compatibility checks.
2) Repeatable gates: contract checks must run i...

title: Progressive Degradation and Quality Fallback Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook defines an operational pattern—progressive request degradation with quality-aware fallbacks—that lets Redwood keep customer-facing LLM features online during transient capacity, hardware, or downstream-service problems while bounding user-visible quality regressions and unit cost. It document...
text:
Overview

This playbook defines an operational pattern—progressive request degradation with quality-aware fallbacks—that lets Redwood keep customer-facing LLM features online during transient capacity, hardware, or downstream-service problems while bounding user-visible quality regressions and unit cost. It documents service-level thresholds, decision logic, implementation knobs, and monitoring / runbook steps.

Goals

- Keep latency-sensitive endpoints responsive under stress with graceful, testable quality reductions.
- Make degradations predictable and auditable: every fallback path must be covered by automated tests, rollout rules, and metrics.
- Optimize for user-perceived utility first, cost second: prefer semantic-preserving fallbacks over blind truncation.
- Provide a single canonical implementation and configuration schema used by hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

title: ADR: Unified Artifact Delivery Protocol for Multimodal Outputs
preview:
Overview:

This ADR defines a unified artifact delivery protocol (UADP v1) for multimodal inference responses across Redwood Inference. The purpose is to provide a single, predictable outbound contract that supports text-only responses, small inline artifacts, and robust streaming/reference-based delivery for large ...
text:
Overview:

This ADR defines a unified artifact delivery protocol (UADP v1) for multimodal inference responses across Redwood Inference. The purpose is to provide a single, predictable outbound contract that supports text-only responses, small inline artifacts, and robust streaming/reference-based delivery for large binary artifacts (images, audio, video, documents).

Motivation:

- Customers increasingly request multimodal outputs. Current ad-hoc encodings (inline base64, multiple multipart variants, private-API-only flows) increase SDK complexity, routing edge cases, and storage/caching ambiguities.
- A common protocol helps with caching, tracing, signature verification, and consistent behavior across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Scope:

This ADR covers: outbound response envelope, inline vs reference delivery semantics, streaming/resume behavior, checksum and provena...

title: ADR: Shadow Traffic Routing Architecture (Inline Mirroring, Bucketing, Fail-Open)
preview:
## Status
Published (approved for Phase 1 MVP). Any material changes to timeouts, bucketing inputs, or request capture fields require a follow-up ADR.

## Summary of decision
We will implement shadow traffic by **inline mirroring** a sampled subset of production requests in the **data plane routing path**, sending a...
text:
## Status
Published (approved for Phase 1 MVP). Any material changes to timeouts, bucketing inputs, or request capture fields require a follow-up ADR.

## Summary of decision
We will implement shadow traffic by **inline mirroring** a sampled subset of production requests in the **data plane routing path**, sending a best-effort duplicate request to one or more shadow targets while returning the **control** response to the user.

Key properties:
- **Fail-open**: shadowing must never block or degrade the user path. Shadow execution is best-effort with strict timeouts and circuit breaking.
- **Deterministic bucketing**: a stable request fingerprint drives sampling and variant assignment for consistent comparisons.
- **Minimal capture**: shadow request/response artifacts are redacted and retained briefly; scoring happens asynchronously.

## Context
Redwood’s Rollouts framework supports ca...

title: Cost-aware streaming payload schema and metadata guideline
preview:
Overview:

This guideline defines a standardized payload schema and runtime metadata conventions for cost-aware streaming inference requests across Redwood’s public and private deployments. It is intended for API engineers, SDK maintainers, runtime routing components, and observability pipelines. The goal is to make...
text:
Overview:

This guideline defines a standardized payload schema and runtime metadata conventions for cost-aware streaming inference requests across Redwood’s public and private deployments. It is intended for API engineers, SDK maintainers, runtime routing components, and observability pipelines. The goal is to make per-request cost signals explicit, machine-readable, and compatible with our streaming and batch transports.

Why this matters:
- Enables accurate per-request cost attribution and billing for streamed tokens and partial outputs.
- Allows runtime routing decisions (e.g., route to cheaper quantized variant) based on declared request constraints.
- Improves observability: deterministic dimensions for SLOs, per-token latencies, and partial-output error attribution.

Scope:
- Applies to: text generation streaming endpoints (server-sent events, chunked HTTP, gRPC streams) and ch...

This document is company-wide and applies to a...
text:
## Purpose
This standard defines Redwood Inference’s minimum requirements for cryptographic signing of release artifacts. The goal is to reduce supply-chain risk by ensuring integrity and provenance of artifacts delivered to customers or used to run customer workloads.

This document is company-wide and applies to all product lines and deployment modes (Hosted, Dedicated, Private). Additional requirements may be layered on by product-specific docs, but may not weaken the controls here.

## Scope
In scope:
- What artifacts must be signed and verified
- Minimum metadata and canonicalization requirements
- Key custody and access controls
- Verification requirements (including “fail-closed” expectations)
- Audit logging and evidence expectations
- Exception / break-glass process

Out of scope:
- Full customer-managed signing root delegation (tracked as a future capability for some Private...

title: ADR: KV cache compaction budgeting (time/bytes moved) and enforcement points

# ADR: KV cache compaction budgeting (time/bytes moved) and enforcement points

## Summary
We will make KV cache compaction **strictly budgeted** and **scheduler-aware**.

**Budgeting dimensions**
- **Time budget (wall-clock):** maximum microseconds of compaction work allowed per scheduling tick.
- **Bytes moved bud...
text:
# ADR: KV cache compaction budgeting (time/bytes moved) and enforcement points

## Summary
We will make KV cache compaction **strictly budgeted** and **scheduler-aware**.

**Budgeting dimensions**
- **Time budget (wall-clock):** maximum microseconds of compaction work allowed per scheduling tick.
- **Bytes moved budget:** maximum bytes of KV pages/blocks relocated per scheduling tick.

**Where budgeting is enforced**
- Primary enforcement is in the **scheduler loop** at defined boundaries (end of prefill, end of decode micro-batch, and/or “idle” windows).
- A **background compaction worker** may run opportunistically, but it is **subordinate** to scheduler budgets and can be preempted/cancelled when the scheduler signals load/latency risk.

This decision is intended to reduce burst-driven tail latency (p95/p99) while still converging fragmentation downward over time.

## Status
**Acce...

text:
## Purpose
This document standardizes **metric names**, **tag sets**, and **reason codes** for overload protection and load shedding across the Hosted API and Dedicated tiers.

The goals are:
- Make overload behavior **explainable and auditable** (for on-call and enterprise escalations).
- Provide consistent dashboards/alerts across gateway, routing, policy engine, and runtime.
- Keep metric cardinality **bounded** while still supporting per-tier and per-route-group SLOs.

This standard applies to:
- API Gateway (request handling + 429 construction)
- Smart Routing (admission control + priority routing)
- Policy Engine (deterministic evaluation + budgets)
- Serving Runtime (queue depth / scheduler lag / backpressure)

---

## Naming conventions
### Metric prefix
All overload protection metrics MUST use the prefix:
- `redwood.overload.*`

This makes it easy to search and to apply unifi...

title: ADR: Hot-route capacity protection — enforcement points (gateway vs routing vs runtime)
preview:
## Status
Published. Effective for Hosted API + Dedicated shared pools.

## Summary
We will implement **hot-route capacity protection** as a **layered (hybrid) enforcement model**:

1) **API Gateway (primary, deterministic):** tier-aware **rate limits + quota evaluation** with consistent 429 semantics and auditable ...
text:
## Status
Published. Effective for Hosted API + Dedicated shared pools.

## Summary
We will implement **hot-route capacity protection** as a **layered (hybrid) enforcement model**:

1) **API Gateway (primary, deterministic):** tier-aware **rate limits + quota evaluation** with consistent 429 semantics and auditable decision logging.
2) **Smart Routing (primary under pressure):** **priority classes + admission control** driven by fleet capacity/queue depth signals; enforce per-route/per-tier budgets during overload.
3) **Serving Runtime (last-resort safety):** **backpressure + controlled shedding** to prevent GPU pool collapse when upstream controls are insufficient or delayed.

This ADR answers: *Where should enforcement live (gateway vs routing vs runtime), and why?*

## Context / Problem
A small number of high-traffic, high-SLA routes ("hot routes" like **/v1/chat/completions**, emb...

title: ADR-042: Failover Trigger Signal Hierarchy (Regional Routing Automation)
preview:
## Status
**Accepted** (effective 2026-01-20)

## Summary
This ADR defines a deterministic hierarchy of trigger signals used to declare a region impaired and initiate automated routing failover for **Hosted API** and **Dedicated** capacity.

Key decisions:
- **Signal priority is fixed** and evaluated deterministical...
text:
## Status
**Accepted** (effective 2026-01-20)

## Summary
This ADR defines a deterministic hierarchy of trigger signals used to declare a region impaired and initiate automated routing failover for **Hosted API** and **Dedicated** capacity.

Key decisions:
- **Signal priority is fixed** and evaluated deterministically: **Reachability > 5xx burn > Tail latency burn > Capacity exhaustion** (with supporting signals and guardrails).
- **Corroboration is required** for all automated failover actions except a narrowly scoped reachability “hard-down” condition.
- **Dampening and hold-down timers** are mandatory to prevent oscillation and cascading overload.
- Failover is **scoped** (route/model/tier) when possible; region-wide failover is reserved for corroborated region-level impairment.

## Context / Problem Statement
Regional failover today is a mix of manual actions and partially automat...

title: ADR: Hardware Tuning Profiles — Distribution, Override Order, and Rollback Semantics
preview:
## Status
Published. Applies to Hosted, Dedicated, and Private.

## Decision summary
We will ship hardware tuning profiles as a **versioned “profile pack”** that is **bundled with the runtime image by default** and can be **optionally overridden by an external bundle**. Profile selection and overrides follow a **str...
text:
## Status
Published. Applies to Hosted, Dedicated, and Private.

## Decision summary
We will ship hardware tuning profiles as a **versioned “profile pack”** that is **bundled with the runtime image by default** and can be **optionally overridden by an external bundle**. Profile selection and overrides follow a **strict precedence order** designed for safety and operability. Rollbacks are defined as **pointer changes** (control plane / deployment config) plus **hard kill-switches** that do not require redeploying workloads.

Key properties:
- **Safe-by-default:** runtime always has a last-known-good pack embedded.
- **Operationally simple:** fleet/pool/cust overrides are declarative; effective config is introspectable.
- **Rollbackable within minutes:** roll back by switching pack version pointers and/or disabling profiles per scope.
- **Supportable:** Private customers can override wi...

title: End-to-End SLI Composition and Cross-Service Correlation Playbook
preview:

The guidance targets hosted, dedicated, and private ...
text:
Summary:
This playbook defines how Redwood composes end-to-end Service Level Indicators (SLIs) for multi-stage inference pipelines and how teams should correlate SLIs with distributed traces and logs to detect regressions and root cause performance issues quickly. The guidance targets hosted, dedicated, and private deployments where requests traverse the API gateway, routing layer, serving runtime, and optional post-processing services.

Goals:
- Provide unambiguous SLI definitions suitable for alerting and SLA reporting.
- Ensure SLIs are computable from existing telemetry within cost and retention constraints.
- Standardize correlation keys and span/metric tags so incidents can be traced end-to-end.
- Offer pragmatic aggregation, bucketing, and sampling rules to balance observability and ingestion cost.

Scope and non-goals:
- Scope: API request paths that produce or route tokens (g...

title: ADR: Demo Tenant Model Pinning and Safe Fallback Policy
preview:

Fallback is enabled only for defined failure modes (capacity...
text:
## Summary
We will pin all demo tenant routes to **immutable model versions** (not “latest” aliases) and layer a **strict, pre-approved fallback matrix** that preserves the demo narrative (latency/cost/reliability) while minimizing surprise output changes. Fallback is enabled only for defined failure modes (capacity, regional impairment, model retirement) and is monitored with alerts.

This ADR is specific to the Redwood Inference **sales demo environment (“demo tenant”)** and does not change production defaults for customer tenants.

---

## Context / Problem
The demo tenant must be:
- **Stable** across model catalog changes, engine upgrades, and region/capacity fluctuations.
- **Representative** of the real product (routing controls, rollouts, reliability behavior, economics tooling).
- **Fast to reset** and safe for parallel demos, with dashboards populated by synthetic traffic.

C...

title: ADR: Short/Long Prompt-Aware Scheduling Policy for Mixed Workloads
preview:
## Status
Published (runtime prototype behind feature flag; rollout gated by SRE guardrails).

## Decision summary
We will implement a **short/long prompt-aware scheduling policy** for the serving runtime to reduce user-visible tail latency under mixed prompt-length distributions.

The policy is implemented as a **c...
text:
## Status
Published (runtime prototype behind feature flag; rollout gated by SRE guardrails).

## Decision summary
We will implement a **short/long prompt-aware scheduling policy** for the serving runtime to reduce user-visible tail latency under mixed prompt-length distributions.

The policy is implemented as a **class-based scheduler (two-queue dispatch)**:
- Classify requests into **SHORT** and **LONG** using prompt/context length and (where available) a lightweight prefill-cost estimate.
- Maintain separate queues and enforce a **dispatch share / token budget** that favors SHORT to protect TTFT and p95 latency.
- Include **aging / starvation prevention** guarantees so LONG requests continue to make progress under sustained SHORT load.

This replaces “single-queue, length-agnostic” selection in mixed workloads while preserving continuous batching.

## Context / problem
Redwood’s co...

title: ADR-043: Automated Routing Policy Rollback Semantics (Anti-flap, Rate Limits, Auditability)
preview:
## Summary
This ADR defines rollback semantics for Redwood’s automated regional failover routing policies. It answers:
- When automation should revert a previously applied routing policy change (rollback / fail-back).
- How we prevent oscillation (“flapping”) under noisy or partial signals.
- Safe-rate limits for po...
text:
## Summary
This ADR defines rollback semantics for Redwood’s automated regional failover routing policies. It answers:
- When automation should revert a previously applied routing policy change (rollback / fail-back).
- How we prevent oscillation (“flapping”) under noisy or partial signals.
- Safe-rate limits for policy flips (global, per-route, per-tenant) to constrain blast radius.
- How the system records/audits decisions so on-call and post-incident reviews can reconstruct who/what/why.

This ADR is complementary to **ADR-042 (Failover Trigger Signal Hierarchy)**. ADR-042 defines *when to fail over*; ADR-043 defines *how to safely reverse those changes* and how to keep the system stable.

## Status
**Accepted** (effective for Hosted API and Dedicated multi-region routing under the Reliability Program).

## Context
Today, regional failover is a mix of manual and partially automated...

title: ADR-0172: Benchmark Metrics and Run Manifest Format (Perf Suite)

1) A canonical set of performance metrics for LLM serving (TTFT, TPOT, and throughput) with strict measurement boundaries.

text:
## Status
Published (effective for Perf Baseline & Representative Workload Suite program starting Q1 2026).

## Decision summary
We standardize:
1) A canonical set of performance metrics for LLM serving (TTFT, TPOT, and throughput) with strict measurement boundaries.
2) A run manifest format (JSON) that captures environment, model configuration, scheduler configuration, workload parameters, and seeds.
3) A minimal results format for exporting per-run aggregates, distributions, and attribution metadata.

This ADR exists to make results reproducible across tools (benchmark-lab harness, perf-canary, runtime microbenches) and attributable across changes (kernel selection, scheduling policies, driver/runtime updates).

## Context and problem
Historically, Redwood teams measured "latency" and "throughput" in different ways:
- TTFT measured as "first token computed" in runtime code vs "first...

title: ADR-0142: Make incident taxonomy a required field in incident tooling
preview:

## Context
Redwood Inference’s Reliability Program is targeting a measurable reduction in incident frequency and MTTR. A recurring blocker has been incon...
text:
## Status
**Accepted / Implementing** (enforcement rollout in incident-bot + metrics started week of 2025-12-16; closure gating for Sev0/Sev1 started 2026-01-06).

## Context
Redwood Inference’s Reliability Program is targeting a measurable reduction in incident frequency and MTTR. A recurring blocker has been inconsistent incident classification:

- Incidents are labeled inconsistently across Slack incident channels, incident-bot records, postmortems, and dashboards.
- Reporting by “service” alone does not reflect operational ownership in practice (e.g., incidents that manifest as 5xx at the API edge but are actually capacity shortages or routing policy failures).
- Freeform tags create high variance and poor aggregation, and they are not enforceable.
- Without consistent classification, we cannot reliably answer:
 - What are the top 10 incident types over the last quarter?
 - Which ...

title: ADR-0173: Representative workload sourcing from production telemetry (privacy-preserving)
preview:
## Status
Published (effective for Q1 2026 perf baseline & representative workload suite v1).

## Context
The “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” program requires a workload suite that is:
1) representative of real Redwood Inference production traffic shapes, and
2) safe to share broadly internally and r...
text:
## Status
Published (effective for Q1 2026 perf baseline & representative workload suite v1).

## Context
The “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” program requires a workload suite that is:
1) representative of real Redwood Inference production traffic shapes, and
2) safe to share broadly internally and runnable in CI/benchmark fleets without exposing customer data.

Historically, ad-hoc benchmarking has used either:
- synthetic workloads that are easy to generate but drift from real traffic, or
- copied customer prompts (explicitly disallowed) that better match real traffic but create privacy, security, and legal risk.

We need a standard method for deriving a stable, repeatable workload definition from production telemetry *without* storing or reconstructing sensitive prompt content.

## Decision
We will derive the representative workload suite from *privacy-preserving pr...

title: ADR-0147: Offline artifact signing scheme for Redwood Private air-gapped bundles
preview:
## Status
Published (effective for Redwood Private offline bundles starting with Private 2026.01 release train).

## Summary
We will cryptographically sign Redwood Private offline bundles by creating a **detached signature** over a **canonicalized bundle manifest**.

**Signing format:** DSSE (Dead Simple Signing Env...
text:
## Status
Published (effective for Redwood Private offline bundles starting with Private 2026.01 release train).

## Summary
We will cryptographically sign Redwood Private offline bundles by creating a **detached signature** over a **canonicalized bundle manifest**.

**Signing format:** DSSE (Dead Simple Signing Envelope) JSON envelope

**Key type / algorithm:** ECDSA P-256 with SHA-256

**Canonicalization:** RFC 8785 JSON Canonicalization Scheme (JCS) over the manifest JSON

**Verification:** The on-prem installer performs signature verification in preflight and **fails closed** on any signature/manifest mismatch (with a documented break-glass path).

This approach is designed to work in **air-gapped** environments, provides clear provenance and auditability, and leaves a migration path toward stronger supply-chain frameworks (in-toto / TUF) without blocking initial delivery.

## Con...

title: ADR-014: Status page component mapping – source of truth and change management
preview:

**Accepted** (effective 2025-11-27)

## Decision drivers
We are upgrading the public status page to be more granular (product surface × region × capability) and to enable incident-bot automation (drafting/publishing updates, accuracy audits). This requires a single, reviewable “source of truth” for:

text:
## Status
**Accepted** (effective 2025-11-27)

## Decision drivers
We are upgrading the public status page to be more granular (product surface × region × capability) and to enable incident-bot automation (drafting/publishing updates, accuracy audits). This requires a single, reviewable “source of truth” for:
- Public component registry (IDs, display names, grouping)
- Mapping rules from internal signals/service tags → public components
- Rollout flags / deprecated components

We must be able to:
- Ship mapping changes safely (avoid misreporting incidents)
- Keep changes reviewable and auditable (who changed what, when)
- Support automation (schema validation + CI)
- Avoid on-call burden and reduce “tribal knowledge”

## Context
Today, component mapping logic is scattered across:
- incident-bot prompts/logic (what fields are required; how components are chosen)
- observability dashboa...

title: Customer-Facing Status Signals Standard (Public Status Page)
preview:
## Purpose
This standard defines which internal health signals are allowed to drive customer-visible component states on the public status page ("Operational", "Degraded Performance", "Partial Outage", "Major Outage") and how to apply thresholds, traffic guards, and anti-flap controls.

Goals:
- Make status page upd...
text:
## Purpose
This standard defines which internal health signals are allowed to drive customer-visible component states on the public status page ("Operational", "Degraded Performance", "Partial Outage", "Major Outage") and how to apply thresholds, traffic guards, and anti-flap controls.

Goals:
- Make status page updates accurate, consistent, and actionable for customers.
- Reduce subjective judgment during incidents by providing default signal gates.
- Prevent flapping (rapid state toggling) and premature recovery.
- Avoid over-promising SLAs or disclosing customer-specific capacity constraints.

Non-goals:
- Replacing human judgment during Sev0/Sev1 incidents.
- Publishing root-cause details or internal service names on the status page.
- Creating per-customer status experiences on the public page.

## Scope
Applies to all public status components defined in the status page component...

title: ADR-015: Admission control layering for hosted overload protection (Gateway vs Routing vs Runtime)
preview:
## Status
Published (effective for Hosted API overload protection rollout).

## Context
Redwood Inference’s Hosted API sees bursty and highly variable traffic (prompt length variance, streaming concurrency spikes, regional capacity dips). During overload, failures can cascade into:
- Tail-latency blowups (p95/p99), ...
text:
## Status
Published (effective for Hosted API overload protection rollout).

## Context
Redwood Inference’s Hosted API sees bursty and highly variable traffic (prompt length variance, streaming concurrency spikes, regional capacity dips). During overload, failures can cascade into:
- Tail-latency blowups (p95/p99), then timeouts
- Elevated 5xx from upstream retry storms and downstream saturation
- Noisy-neighbor effects across tenants and tiers
- Confusing customer experience (timeouts and generic 5xx instead of stable semantics)

We need a layered admission control strategy that:
1) Rejects or degrades traffic early and predictably.
2) Preserves tenant isolation and tier-aware behavior.
3) Avoids feedback loops (e.g., shedding causing routing thrash, or routing failover amplifying load elsewhere).
4) Produces consistent customer-facing overload errors (see ADR-014).

This ADR defines...

title: ADR-008: Key rotation strategy (key versioning, dual-read/dual-decrypt window, cutover + rollback)
preview:
## Status
Published.

## Summary
This ADR standardizes how Redwood rotates encryption keys used for envelope encryption across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. The core decision is to use explicit *key versioning* plus a bounded *dual-read/dual-decrypt* window controlled by configuration flags, with clear...
text:
## Status
Published.

## Summary
This ADR standardizes how Redwood rotates encryption keys used for envelope encryption across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. The core decision is to use explicit *key versioning* plus a bounded *dual-read/dual-decrypt* window controlled by configuration flags, with clear cutover criteria and rollback steps.

The strategy is designed to:
- Avoid hard cutovers that cause widespread decrypt failures.
- Provide auditable, repeatable rotation steps (including evidence artifacts).
- Limit blast radius during KMS incidents by controlling retry budgets and fallback behavior (see ADR-007).

## Context
Redwood uses envelope encryption for sensitive material (e.g., stored credentials, connectors, some control-plane configuration secrets, and other scoped data that is encrypted-at-rest outside of core database encryption). In envelope encryption:
- A ...

title: ADR-014: Hosted overload error taxonomy (customer-facing)
preview:
## Summary
This ADR defines the **customer-facing error taxonomy** for overload protection in the Redwood Hosted API, including:
- Stable **error codes** and their semantics
- HTTP **status mapping** (primarily 429 vs 503)
- Standard JSON **response payload shape** (additive and backwards compatible)
- **Retry-After...
text:
## Summary
This ADR defines the **customer-facing error taxonomy** for overload protection in the Redwood Hosted API, including:
- Stable **error codes** and their semantics
- HTTP **status mapping** (primarily 429 vs 503)
- Standard JSON **response payload shape** (additive and backwards compatible)
- **Retry-After** header semantics and client retry guidance

This taxonomy is used by the API Gateway admission layer, Smart Routing, and serving runtime admission points.

## Context / Problem
The Hosted API experiences bursty workloads. During saturation, failures have historically presented as ambiguous timeouts and generic 5xx errors, causing:
- Retry storms and further overload
- Confusing customer experience (unclear whether to retry, when, and how)
- Difficult incident triage due to inconsistent error signatures across services

We need **stable, explicit overload errors** so:
- T...

title: ADR-007: Standard KMS Client Wrapper and Failure Policy

## Status
Published.

We will standardize all Key Management Service (KMS/HSM-backed) calls in the Redwood monorepo behind a shared **KMS client wrapper** with:
- Explicit, consistent **timeouts** and **retry budgets** (with jitter/backoff).

text:
## Status
Published.

## Summary
We will standardize all Key Management Service (KMS/HSM-backed) calls in the Redwood monorepo behind a shared **KMS client wrapper** with:
- Explicit, consistent **timeouts** and **retry budgets** (with jitter/backoff).
- A canonical **error taxonomy** used across logs, metrics, traces, and user-facing/operator-facing messages.
- A shared **circuit breaker** and **backpressure** policy to prevent retry storms and cascading failures when KMS is degraded.
- Strict **caching constraints** and safe defaults appropriate for envelope encryption.

This ADR defines the wrapper API shape, failure-handling rules, observability requirements, and non-goals.

## Context / Problem
Redwood’s Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments all integrate with customer-managed KMS/HSM for envelope encryption flows (e.g., DEK generation/encryption, DEK decryption, key metadat...

title: ADR: Safe Apply Gating Signals for Optimization Config Rollouts
preview:

## Context
Redwood Optimize allows high-impact runtime configuration changes (batching heuristics, caching toggles/knobs, and quantization profiles). Hi...
text:
## Status
Published (effective for Hosted + Dedicated rollouts). Private deployments must implement equivalent metrics and can use the same thresholds as defaults.

## Context
Redwood Optimize allows high-impact runtime configuration changes (batching heuristics, caching toggles/knobs, and quantization profiles). Historically, config changes have caused:
- Latency regressions (p95/p99 spikes) that were not detected during early rollout steps.
- Elevated 5xx/timeout rates due to semantic misconfiguration or resource exhaustion.
- "Silent" quality regressions with quantization profiles that only surfaced in downstream customer behavior.
- Operational ambiguity during incidents (unclear whether to pause, continue, or rollback) due to inconsistent metrics and missing baselines.

This ADR defines **the gating signals** used by the Safe Apply controller to determine whether a staged rollout...

title: ADR: Optimization Config Versioning Model (Immutable Versions + Scoped Pointers)
preview:
## Status
Published (approved in Architecture Review on 2025-10-21).

## Context
Redwood Optimize supports a set of operational inference optimizations (batching heuristics, caching toggles/parameters, and quantization profiles). Historically, optimization changes have been applied as mutable configuration updates. ...
text:
## Status
Published (approved in Architecture Review on 2025-10-21).

## Context
Redwood Optimize supports a set of operational inference optimizations (batching heuristics, caching toggles/parameters, and quantization profiles). Historically, optimization changes have been applied as mutable configuration updates. This has caused:
- Limited traceability (difficult to answer “what changed, by whom, and where is it active?”).
- Higher change-failure risk (hard to stage safely across tenant/model/region).
- Incomplete rollback semantics (partial worker apply, race conditions, and unclear “current” config).

This ADR defines the **versioning model** for Optimization Configs in the control plane, including:
- Immutable version objects vs mutable config objects.
- How “active config” is represented (pointers/bindings).
- Promotion semantics and environment scoping.

The model must support ...

title: ADR-017: Provision-to-Ready Canonical Readiness State Machine
preview:

Canonical states:
- **Allocated**
- **Provision...
text:
## Status
Published (effective immediately for all Dedicated provisioning automation).

## Decision summary
We will use a single, canonical readiness state machine for GPU worker nodes as they move from capacity allocation to being eligible to run inference workloads.

Canonical states:
- **Allocated**
- **Provisioning**
- **Validating**
- **Ready**
- **Quarantined**

The **Fleet Readiness Service (control-plane)** is the source of truth for the current state. State transitions are driven by an orchestrator and validated by authoritative signals (agent heartbeat + validation results) with strict rules to prevent accidental promotion.

## Context / problem
Historically, “node readiness” was inferred from a mix of:
- cloud provider instance status,
- Kubernetes node conditions,
- ad-hoc provisioning scripts,
- and operator judgment.

This created ambiguity (e.g., “node is up” vs “node i...

title: Model Metadata Schema v1 (Model Registry + Serving)
preview:
## Purpose
This document defines **Model Metadata Schema v1** used by the Model Registry and Control Plane to:
- Provide a single source of truth for **served model identity**, **versioning**, **compatibility**, and **deployment availability**.
- Enable automated **customer-visible release notes** generation (Consol...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines **Model Metadata Schema v1** used by the Model Registry and Control Plane to:
- Provide a single source of truth for **served model identity**, **versioning**, **compatibility**, and **deployment availability**.
- Enable automated **customer-visible release notes** generation (Console + docs) without leaking internal-only details.
- Support internal auditability (who changed what, when, and what was promoted) and safe rollout policy evaluation.

Schema v1 is intentionally scoped to information needed for **serving** (runtime/tokenizer/limits/features), **rollouts** (eligibility/risk/deprecations), and **change visibility** (public summaries + internal rationale/evidence pointers).

## Scope
### In scope
- Model + variant + quantization profile identity and relationships
- SemVer model versioning rules for served endpoints
- Compatibility semantics and ...

title: Audit log export (customer-managed destinations): architecture and operational model
preview:
# Overview
Redwood Inference supports **customer-managed audit log export** so customers can ingest Redwood audit events into their SIEM (Splunk, Datadog, Chronicle, Sentinel, etc.) from a destination they control.

This page describes the reference architecture used across deployment modes (Hosted, Dedicated, Priva...
text:
# Overview
Redwood Inference supports **customer-managed audit log export** so customers can ingest Redwood audit events into their SIEM (Splunk, Datadog, Chronicle, Sentinel, etc.) from a destination they control.

This page describes the reference architecture used across deployment modes (Hosted, Dedicated, Private) and how responsibilities are split between Redwood and the customer.

# Goals
- Provide near-real-time delivery of **platform audit logs** to customer storage.
- Ensure exports are secure (least privilege + encryption) and observable (failure detection + retry).
- Keep a consistent schema across modes and regions.

# Non-goals
- Exporting inference payloads (prompt/completion).
- Providing customer-specific transformations beyond supported formats.

# Components
## 1) Audit Event Producers
Sources of events (examples):
- `control-plane-auth` (SSO, API key lifecycle, tok...

title: ADR-0142: Model metadata source of truth and publishing workflow (public vs internal fields)

Published.

## Context
Redwood serves models across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. Today, model/version information is spread across:
- Model registry records (catalog, identifiers, some versioning)
- Control plane runtime config (availability, routing, rollouts, regions)

text:
## Status
Published.

## Context
Redwood serves models across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. Today, model/version information is spread across:
- Model registry records (catalog, identifiers, some versioning)
- Control plane runtime config (availability, routing, rollouts, regions)
- Release engineering and docs processes (manual notes, inconsistent categories)

This fragmentation has created two classes of issues:
1) **Customer-facing change visibility gaps**: promotions and changes sometimes happen without consistent, discoverable release notes (Console + docs). Customers cannot reliably answer “what changed / why / risk / rollback”.
2) **Internal auditability gaps**: support and compliance need to reconstruct “who changed what, when, and where” across deployment modes, without leaking sensitive details to standard users.

We need a single, consistent source of truth fo...

text:
## Status
Published (effective starting with Model Metadata Schema v1 rollout).

## Context
Redwood Inference is standardizing model/version metadata and generating customer-visible release notes for model/variant/quantization profile changes.

We need a reliable, auditable source for changelog generation that supports:
- Customer-visible release notes (Console + docs site)
- Internal auditability (who changed what/when/where)
- Safe rollout policies (canary, A/B, promotion, rollback)
- Multi-deployment reality (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private timing differences)

Historically, change visibility has been fragmented:
- Some change info lives in the model registry records
- Promotions happen through control plane workflows
- Human-authored release notes are sometimes captured via GitHub PR text/labels

This ADR decides the primary source-of-truth for changelog generation and the role of ...

text:
h1. Purpose
This standard defines what must be true for a model version to be considered production-ready on Redwood Inference. Rollout plans MUST reference the readiness artifacts listed here.

h1. Definitions
* Model version: immutable build of weights + tokenizer + runtime settings, identified by a version id.
* Compatibility: whether a version can serve a given request type (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank) and feature set (streaming, tool calling, structured output).

h1. Required readiness artifacts (all production versions)
A model version is READY_FOR_PROD only when the following are complete and linked in the model registry entry:

h2. A) Functional compatibility
* Supported request types: chat, completions, embeddings, rerank (list explicitly)
* Streaming support: yes/no and known limitations
* Tool/function calling: supported schemas and known differences
* Structured ou...

text:
## Purpose
This page standardizes metric naming and labeling for scaling and capacity features across Redwood Inference, with immediate focus on **Dedicated** autoscaling bounds and predictive scaling signals.

Goals:
- Make dashboards and alerts consistent across services/regions.
- Enable deterministic auditability of scaling behavior (via metrics + logs) without exploding metric cardinality.
- Establish a clear contract for which dimensions are allowed on **service-level** metrics vs **customer/deployment-level** metrics.

Non-goals:
- This document does not define exact alert thresholds or SLO targets (see related SRE pages).
- This document does not replace event/audit logging requirements (see audit event schema pages).

---

## Metric backend assumptions
- Metrics are **Prometheus-compatible** (direct Prometheus client or via OTel Collector).
- Naming and types follow Prometheu...

title: Metrics & Alerting Standards for Redwood Private (Prometheus/Grafana)
preview:

This document defines the **minimum supported** standards for metrics and alerting shipped with **Redwood Private** (VPC and on-prem). The goal is to ensure:
- Customers can monitor health and performance without bespoke work.
- Alerts are actionable and map cleanly to SLOs and common failure modes.
- Met...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines the **minimum supported** standards for metrics and alerting shipped with **Redwood Private** (VPC and on-prem). The goal is to ensure:
- Customers can monitor health and performance without bespoke work.
- Alerts are actionable and map cleanly to SLOs and common failure modes.
- Metrics remain safe for customer-run Prometheus (bounded cardinality, predictable resource usage).
- Dashboards and alerts remain stable across upgrades.

These standards apply to all in-scope Private components:
- Private control plane services (config/policy, auth integration points)
- API gateway
- Routing/policy engine
- Serving runtime pods
- Model registry integration points
- Installer/upgrade controller

---

## Supported stack (v1 minimum pack)
Redwood Private v1 minimum pack assumes:
- **Prometheus** (or Prometheus-compatible scrape + rule evaluation)
- **Alertmanage...

title: Terraform Module Versioning and Release Process (Alpha → GA)
preview:
## Purpose
This standard defines how Redwood Inference versions, tags, and releases Terraform modules in the `redwood-terraform` repository. It also defines changelog discipline, compatibility commitments, and the support contract differences between **alpha** and **GA**.

This document exists to keep Private deploy...
text:
## Purpose
This standard defines how Redwood Inference versions, tags, and releases Terraform modules in the `redwood-terraform` repository. It also defines changelog discipline, compatibility commitments, and the support contract differences between **alpha** and **GA**.

This document exists to keep Private deployment pilots predictable and supportable while we iterate quickly.

## Scope
**In scope**
- All Terraform modules shipped from `redwood-terraform` (e.g., VPC, endpoints, logging, security groups, IAM scaffolding, examples).
- Versioning, release tags, changelog requirements, and compatibility declarations.
- Commitments for alpha vs GA and how we communicate breaking changes.

**Out of scope**
- End-to-end cluster/application installation (handled by `redwood-private-installer` and Helm).
- Cloud-provider-specific release processes outside AWS (GCP/Azure stubs only in alpha)...

title: ADR-047: Dedicated Autoscaling Bounds — Precedence and Safety Invariants
preview:

This ADR defines the **authoritative semantics** for customer-configurable autoscaling bounds (min/max) for **Redwood Dedicated** deployments, including:
- Precedence between customer bounds and platform defaults
- Conflict resolution between **predictive** and **reactive** scaling signals
- Safety invari...
text:
## Summary
This ADR defines the **authoritative semantics** for customer-configurable autoscaling bounds (min/max) for **Redwood Dedicated** deployments, including:
- Precedence between customer bounds and platform defaults
- Conflict resolution between **predictive** and **reactive** scaling signals
- Safety invariants for scale-down (and scale-up) behavior
- Deterministic evaluation requirements for auditability

The goal is to provide enterprise-grade, contract-aligned guardrails that are **hard enforced**, **observable**, and **operationally safe**.

---

## Status
**Accepted** (implemented behind feature flags; required for GA).

---

## Context
Redwood Dedicated historically relies on platform-driven autoscaling (reactive metrics + operator overrides). Enterprise customers require:
- A **hard maximum** to prevent unexpected spend and enforce contractual ceilings.
- A **hard mini...

title: ADR-022: Log pipeline defaults for Redwood Private (collector, format, redaction, sinks)
preview:
## Status
**Accepted / Published**

## Decision summary
For Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped) we will ship a **supported default log collection and forwarding configuration** with the Private installer/Helm charts.

**We will:**
1) Standardize Redwood service operational logs as **JSON structured logs** wit...
text:
## Status
**Accepted / Published**

## Decision summary
For Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped) we will ship a **supported default log collection and forwarding configuration** with the Private installer/Helm charts.

**We will:**
1) Standardize Redwood service operational logs as **JSON structured logs** with an allowlisted schema.
2) Deploy **Vector** (Timber.io) as the default log collector/forwarder in Private installs.
3) Implement a **defense-in-depth redaction strategy** (application-side + collector-side filtering) with conservative defaults.
4) Support a small set of **first-class sinks** via documented, tested configurations:
 - **Loki** (Grafana Loki) (in-cluster or remote)
 - **Elasticsearch/OpenSearch**
 - **Splunk HEC**
 - **OTLP logs** (to a customer-provided OpenTelemetry Collector / backend)

We will not ship a bespoke SIEM; we will provide export configuration...

title: ADR-027: Terraform module boundaries and public interface for Redwood Private VPC (alpha)

The module suite is intentionally **opin...
text:
## Summary
This ADR defines the module boundaries and the public inputs/outputs for the **Redwood Private VPC Terraform modules (alpha)**. The goal is to encode Redwood-validated defaults for AWS networking + foundational infrastructure while remaining supportable in pilots.

The module suite is intentionally **opinionated**. Flexibility is provided via constrained inputs and explicit extension points rather than “infinite knobs.”

---

**Accepted** (alpha)

- Decision date: **2025-06-06**
- Effective for: `redwood-terraform` v0.x (alpha)
- Owners: Infra (primary) + Private Deployments (constraints) + Security (defaults)

---

## Context / Problem
Enterprise and regulated customers frequently have bespoke VPC layouts, DNS, egress controls, and logging requirements. During technical discovery and pilots, variability in customer setups repeatedly causes:

- Installer failures ...

title: ADR-028: Validation and integration test strategy for Terraform (VPC modules alpha)
preview:
## Status
Accepted (published)

## Decision summary
We will implement a two-tier validation strategy for the `redwood-terraform` module suite:

1) **Fast, deterministic static checks on every PR** (format, validation, lint, security scanning, docs generation drift).
2) **Ephemeral AWS integration tests** that create...
text:
## Status
Accepted (published)

## Decision summary
We will implement a two-tier validation strategy for the `redwood-terraform` module suite:

1) **Fast, deterministic static checks on every PR** (format, validation, lint, security scanning, docs generation drift).
2) **Ephemeral AWS integration tests** that create and destroy real infrastructure in a sandbox account to validate module composition and runtime assumptions (routing, endpoints/DNS behavior, log destinations, and IAM scaffolding).

Integration tests will be split into:
- **PR-gated smoke tests** (small footprint, low cost, <30 minutes) for changes that touch high-risk modules.
- **Nightly full-suite tests** (broader coverage, higher cost, up to ~2 hours) to catch drift and cross-module regressions.

Changes are accepted only if they meet the acceptance criteria defined in this ADR (see: “Acceptance criteria / merge gates...

title: ADR: Private upgrade plan architecture + rollback contract
preview:
## Status
Published (effective starting Private release train 2025.06).

## Decision summary
We will implement Private upgrades as a **two-step, plan-driven workflow**:
1) **Generate** a deterministic, versioned **Upgrade Plan** (declarative plan artifact) after running preflight validation and artifact availability...
text:
## Status
Published (effective starting Private release train 2025.06).

## Decision summary
We will implement Private upgrades as a **two-step, plan-driven workflow**:
1) **Generate** a deterministic, versioned **Upgrade Plan** (declarative plan artifact) after running preflight validation and artifact availability checks.
2) **Execute** the plan using the installer state machine, persisting step outcomes and emitting audit events.

Rollback will be supported through a **defined rollback contract**:
- Rollback is executed by consuming the persisted upgrade state + plan.
- Rollback is **best-effort and deterministic** within the supported contract (see “Rollback contract”).
- We will provide explicit, operator-friendly “manual intervention required” guidance for scenarios outside the contract.

We explicitly reject an imperative-only “do upgrade now” approach and an “always blue/green...

title: ADR: HubSpot routing and ownership rules for PLG signals (v1)
preview:
## Summary
This ADR defines how Redwood routes qualified PLG-to-sales signals into HubSpot, including deterministic company ownership assignment, task creation rules, re-notification thresholds, and auditability requirements. The goal is to create timely, actionable follow-up for Dedicated/Private expansion while mi...
text:
## Summary
This ADR defines how Redwood routes qualified PLG-to-sales signals into HubSpot, including deterministic company ownership assignment, task creation rules, re-notification thresholds, and auditability requirements. The goal is to create timely, actionable follow-up for Dedicated/Private expansion while minimizing spam, avoiding conflicting outreach with CS/Marketing, and ensuring decisions are explainable and reproducible.

## Context
The PLG signal pipeline produces a scored, deduped set of "sales-worthy" signals (usage thresholds and intent signals). Once a signal crosses the routing threshold, we:
- Update HubSpot Company properties to reflect the latest signal state.
- Append a HubSpot timeline note summarizing the signal.
- Create a follow-up task with clear next steps and due dates.
- Assign the Company (and the task) to the correct owner.

Key constraints:
- HubSpot ...

title: ADR-021: Private Observability Minimum Pack — Scope, Stack Dependencies, and Export Integrations
preview:
## Status
Published (effective starting Redwood Private release line: 2025.07)

## Context
Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem) deployments have historically had bespoke observability setups depending on the customer’s cluster standards and their preferred tooling. This creates recurring issues:
- Support/SRE cannot rely...
text:
## Status
Published (effective starting Redwood Private release line: 2025.07)

## Context
Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem) deployments have historically had bespoke observability setups depending on the customer’s cluster standards and their preferred tooling. This creates recurring issues:
- Support/SRE cannot rely on consistent signals, dashboards, or alert semantics across installs.
- Customers experience time-to-diagnose delays (missing dashboards, inconsistent metric names, no runbook links, noisy/unbounded alerts).
- Air-gapped/on-prem installs require deterministic packaging of artifacts (dashboards/rules/config), which ad-hoc setups do not provide.

The “Private Observability Minimum Pack” project standardizes the lowest viable, supported observability bundle that ships with Redwood Private via Helm charts and the private installer.

This ADR defines:
- What is in scope vs out...

title: Burst policy, SLA smoothing, and simulated loadbook
preview:
SUMMARY
This playbook describes a unified approach for handling traffic bursts against latency SLOs using three coordinated mechanisms: (1) burst admission policies, (2) SLA smoothing (adaptive target windows and error-budget shaping), and (3) simulated-load scenarios for verification and capacity planning. The goal...
text:
SUMMARY
This playbook describes a unified approach for handling traffic bursts against latency SLOs using three coordinated mechanisms: (1) burst admission policies, (2) SLA smoothing (adaptive target windows and error-budget shaping), and (3) simulated-load scenarios for verification and capacity planning. The goal is to keep P95 and P99 latency SLOs predictable across short-term spikes while limiting cost and preserving error budgets for sustained load.

Scope and audience
- Scope: inference-router, cost-aware-batcher, model-variant-fallback, and the traffic-shaper service.
- Audience: Platform SREs, Serving Runtime, Performance Engineering, and Product engineers owning route-level SLOs.

Motivation
Large variance in upstream request arrival patterns (SDK retries, client-side misconfiguration, bursty user behavior) causes repeated SLO breaches even when average utilization is within...

title: ADR: Usage + Intent Signals Schema v1 (Canonical PLG Signal Object)
preview:

## Context
Redwood is implementing a privacy-aware pipeline that detects product-led signals (usage thresholds + buying intent), scores them, and routes qualified items into HubSpot for follow-up.

We ...
text:
## Status
**Accepted (v1).** Implemented in the signal aggregation + routing service and used for HubSpot routing.

## Context
Redwood is implementing a privacy-aware pipeline that detects product-led signals (usage thresholds + buying intent), scores them, and routes qualified items into HubSpot for follow-up.

We need a **canonical, versioned signal object** that:
- Works for both **threshold** signals (e.g., spend velocity, sustained tokens, 429 rate) and **intent** signals (e.g., Dedicated/Private docs interest, security FAQ views).
- Supports **aggregation windows** (daily/weekly/30d), **deduping**, and **suppression** (to reduce spam).
- Allows deterministic **scoring** + routing, with auditability.
- Minimizes sensitive data, aligning with the PLG signal data handling policy.

## Decision
Adopt a single canonical record called **PLG Signal (schema v1)** emitted by the scoring s...

title: Incident context collection guidelines (Incident Bot v2 + diagnostics tooling)
preview:
## Purpose
This document standardizes *what* context we collect in the first 5 minutes of an incident and *how* we collect it safely and reliably.

Primary consumers:
- Incident Bot v2 (Slack cards posted in **#incidents** and **#eng-oncall**)
- Diagnostics endpoints/services that power the bot and Console deep link...
text:
## Purpose
This document standardizes *what* context we collect in the first 5 minutes of an incident and *how* we collect it safely and reliably.

Primary consumers:
- Incident Bot v2 (Slack cards posted in **#incidents** and **#eng-oncall**)
- Diagnostics endpoints/services that power the bot and Console deep links
- Humans (on-call, SRE, platform, telemetry) who need consistent signals across hosted/dedicated/private environments

Non-goals:
- Defining a full incident management process (see the incident process pages)
- Replacing runbooks (this doc links to runbooks and provides consistent field/link conventions)

---

## Design principles
1) **Actionable > exhaustive.** Context should support a decision within minutes (rollback? failover? load-shed? paging escalation?), not be a data dump.
2) **Bounded and reliable under failure.** Diagnostics must not amplify an incident (query ...

title: ADR-018: Audit Export Delivery Semantics and Idempotency
preview:
## Status
Published.

## Decision drivers
Enterprise customers need audit events exported into SIEM tooling (Splunk, Datadog, and generic S3 delivery) with delivery guarantees suitable for compliance evidence and incident response. We must support retries/backfill without losing events, while minimizing operational ...
text:
## Status
Published.

## Decision drivers
Enterprise customers need audit events exported into SIEM tooling (Splunk, Datadog, and generic S3 delivery) with delivery guarantees suitable for compliance evidence and incident response. We must support retries/backfill without losing events, while minimizing operational risk (runaway retries, duplication storms) and avoiding requirements that cannot be met reliably across heterogeneous sinks.

This ADR defines:
- Delivery semantics (what we guarantee, and what we explicitly do *not* guarantee)
- The idempotency strategy (how duplicates are identified and handled)
- The checkpoint model (how we ensure continuity and recoverability)
- Replay/backfill semantics (how we re-send historical data safely)
- Downstream expectations (how customers should deduplicate)

## Context
### Terminology
- **Audit event**: A single canonical event emitted by ...

title: ADR: Audit Export Filtering Model (SIEM-ready audit export beta)
preview:
## Status
Published.

## Context
Redwood is building a beta “SIEM-ready audit export” capability with configurable filtering so enterprise customers can:
- Reduce noise/cost in downstream SIEMs
- Target specific compliance-relevant event types
- Minimize exposure of potentially sensitive event content by exporting o...
text:
## Status
Published.

## Context
Redwood is building a beta “SIEM-ready audit export” capability with configurable filtering so enterprise customers can:
- Reduce noise/cost in downstream SIEMs
- Target specific compliance-relevant event types
- Minimize exposure of potentially sensitive event content by exporting only what is needed

The exporter is designed for at-least-once delivery with retries/backoff and checkpointing. Filtering interacts with delivery guarantees and operational behavior:
- Filters must be deterministic and stable across retries/replays.
- Filters must not introduce unbounded CPU/memory overhead in the exporter.
- Filters must be safe to expose in the Console (admin workflows) and safe to evaluate at high event volumes.

This ADR defines (1) the filter model and semantics (allowlist/denylist, event categories, actor/resource predicates, time windows), (2) where ...
